entities.name: Jubertus of Bavaria
entities.raw_quote: One of the many subjects brought before the judges in this campaign was a sixty-year-old man, Jubertus of Bavaria, from Regensburg, tried in 1437 at Briançon.

entities.name: Johannes Cunalis
entities.raw_quote: …he served a certain powerful man in Bavaria who was called Johannes Cunalis, who is a priest and plebanus, in a city called Munich in Bavaria, near Bohemia.

entities.name: Luxuriosus
entities.raw_quote: ...when he who spoke opened this book at once there appeared to him three demons, one named Luxuriosus...

entities.name: Superbus
entities.raw_quote: ...another named Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils.

entities.name: Avarus
entities.raw_quote: ...another named Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils.

entities.name: CLM 849
entities.raw_quote: The fact that our necromantic manuscript is now in the Bavarian State Library... Clm 849...

entities.name: librum de nigromancia
entities.raw_quote: ...this Johannes Cunalis had a book of necromancy [librum de nigromancia]...

entities.name: Das půch aller verpoten kunst
entities.raw_quote: Hartlieb... wrote Das půch aller verpoten kunst (The Book of All Forbidden Arts) at the behest of Margrave Johann...

entities.name: Johannes Hartlieb

attributes.role: physician to Duke of Bavaria, author

entities.raw_quote: Hartlieb served the duke of Bavaria in Munich during the last three decades of his life...

entities.name: necromancy (nigramancia)
entities.raw_quote: Hartlieb lists necromancy (nigramancia) as the first of the seven forbidden arts...

entities.name: verpintnuß
entities.raw_quote: ...must make an oath and pact [verpintnuß] with the devils.

entities.name: Isidore of Seville

attributes.classification of magic: four species of divination by elements

entities.raw_quote: Isidore of Seville, who deals with magic and especially magical divination in his Etymologies...

entities.name: Hugh of St Victor
entities.raw_quote: Hugh of St Victor’s Didascalicon borrows from Isidore...

entities.name: Nicholas Magni of Jawor

attributes.influence: on Hartlieb and on categorization of forbidden arts

entities.raw_quote: ...influenced in particular by Nicholas Magni of Jawor...

entities.name: Johannes Reuchlin
entities.raw_quote: ...Humanist mages such as Johannes Reuchlin protested that the debased magic... could have no effect because the very names they used for the spirits were corrupt...

entities.name: Jamnes and Mambres
entities.raw_quote: The formula ‘May your arts fail … as Jamnes and Mambres failed’...

entities.name: Michael Scot
entities.raw_quote: A closer model might be Michael Scot, who served at the court of Frederick II and had an interest in astrology...

summary: This chunk situates the Munich Handbook (CLM 849) in its historical context, focusing on late medieval necromancy in and around Munich. It discusses the trial of Jubertus of Bavaria, who confessed to conjuring three demons—Luxuriosus, Superbus, and Avarus—from a book owned by the priest Johannes Cunalis. The chapter also examines Johannes Hartlieb’s classification of necromancy as the first forbidden art and his description of demonic pacts and rituals. It explores possible links between Hartlieb, CLM 849, and clerical magical practice, while analyzing the manuscript’s compilation process and scribal characteristics.
key_passages: ...when he who spoke opened this book at once there appeared to him three demons, one named Luxuriosus, another Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils.
Hartlieb lists necromancy (nigramancia) as the first of the seven forbidden arts, along with geomancy, hydromancy, aeromancy, pyromancy, chiromancy and spatulamancy.
...one who wishes to exercise this art must give all sorts of sacrifices to the devils, and must make an oath and pact [verpintnuß] with the devils.
The writer’s own orthography was highly erratic; within the same experiment he sometimes slipped from one version to another in his names for demons...
What seems quite clear is that the writer was a cleric – probably a priest, and at least a person in minor orders.
